quiz_questions.question_text: Evaluate P(8, 3).
Evaluate C(10, 4).
How many ways can 5 people be seated in a row of 5 chairs?
How many ways can you choose 3 books from a shelf of 7?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order doesn't matter: C(7,3) = 7!/(3! · 4!) = 35.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many distinguishable arrangements of the letters in MISSISSIPPI?
Evaluate P(6, 2).
Evaluate C(6, 6).
quiz_questions.explanation: C(6,6) = 6!/(6! · 0!) = 1. There's only one way to choose all items.

quiz_questions.question_text: In how many ways can a committee of 4 be chosen from 9 people?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order doesn't matter for a committee: C(9,4) = 9!/(4! · 5!) = 126.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many ways can you arrange 3 out of 10 runners in 1st, 2nd, 3rd place?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order matters: P(10,3) = 10 × 9 × 8 = 720.

quiz_questions.question_text: Evaluate C(5, 0).
quiz_questions.explanation: C(5,0) = 5!/(0! · 5!) = 1. There is exactly one way to choose nothing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a permutation?

options.text: A selection of items where order does NOT matter
An arrangement of items where order DOES matter

quiz_questions.explanation: A permutation is an ordered arrangement. The order of selection matters, so AB is different from BA.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a combination?

options.text: A selection of items where order does NOT matter
The total number of possible outcomes

quiz_questions.explanation: A combination is a selection where order doesn't matter. Choosing {A, B, C} is the same as {C, B, A}.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for P(n, r)?
quiz_questions.explanation: P(n,r) = n!/(n−r)! counts ordered arrangements of r items chosen from n.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for C(n, r)?
quiz_questions.explanation: C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n−r)!) counts unordered selections of r items from n. It equals P(n,r)/r!.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'permutation with repetition'?

options.text: Arranging items where some are identical, using n!/(n₁! · n₂! · ...)
Choosing items without regard to order
Selecting items that can be reused

quiz_questions.explanation: When some items are identical (repeated), the number of distinguishable arrangements is n!/(n₁! · n₂! · ...) where nᵢ counts each group of identical items.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you decide whether a problem requires a permutation or a combination?

options.text: Use a permutation if n > 10, otherwise use a combination
If the order of selection matters, use a permutation; if not, use a combination
Always use a combination for real-world problems
Use permutation only for numbers, combination only for letters

quiz_questions.explanation: The key distinction: if rearranging the selected items creates a different outcome (like 1st/2nd place), use a permutation. If not (like choosing committee members), use a combination.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the relationship between P(n, r) and C(n, r)?
quiz_questions.explanation: C(n,r) = P(n,r)/r! because combinations remove the r! orderings of each selection that permutations count separately.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does C(n, r) = C(n, n−r) mean?

options.text: Choosing r items is the same as leaving out n−r items
The formula only works when r = n/2
Permutations and combinations are the same

quiz_questions.explanation: This symmetry property means choosing r items from n is equivalent to choosing which n−r items to leave out. For example, C(10,3) = C(10,7).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'distinguishable' mean in the context of permutations?

options.text: All items are unique and can be told apart

quiz_questions.explanation: Distinguishable means each item is unique and can be identified separately. When items are indistinguishable (identical), we divide by the repeated factorial.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'circular permutation'?

options.text: An arrangement of items in a circle where rotations are considered the same
Any arrangement of items in a line

quiz_questions.explanation: In circular permutations, arrangements that differ only by rotation are identical. The number of circular permutations of n items is (n−1)!.

quiz_questions.question_text: A basketball coach needs to choose 5 starters from a roster of 12 players. How many different starting lineups are possible?
quiz_questions.explanation: Order doesn't matter for a starting lineup: C(12,5) = 12!/(5! · 7!) = 792.

quiz_questions.question_text: A DJ has 20 songs and must create an ordered playlist of 4 songs. How many different playlists are possible?
A lock combination requires 3 numbers from 0 to 39 (repetition allowed). How many possible combinations exist?
quiz_questions.explanation: With repetition: 40 × 40 × 40 = 64,000. (Note: Despite being called a 'combination lock,' order matters.)

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher must select 3 students from a class of 25 to attend a conference. How many selections are possible?
A store displays 8 different products in a row in their window. How many different display arrangements are possible?
quiz_questions.explanation: All 8 arranged in a row: 8! = 40,320.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pizza menu offers 10 toppings. How many different 3-topping pizzas can be made (order of toppings doesn't matter)?
How many distinguishable arrangements are there of the letters in the word BOOKKEEPER?
A hand of 5 cards is dealt from a standard 52-card deck. How many possible hands are there?
quiz_questions.explanation: C(52,5) = 52!/(5! · 47!) = 2,598,960. Order of cards in a hand doesn't matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school play needs to assign 4 specific roles from 15 auditioners. How many casting choices are there?
quiz_questions.explanation: Each role is distinct (order matters): P(15,4) = 15 × 14 × 13 × 12 = 32,760.

quiz_questions.question_text: A florist makes bouquets by choosing 4 flowers from 9 varieties for the main display, then arranges them left to right. How many distinct bouquets can be made?
quiz_questions.explanation: Since arrangement matters (left to right): P(9,4) = 9 × 8 × 7 × 6 = 3,024.
